# Diffusion Policy {.section-divider}

## What Diffusion Policy Generates

Diffusion Policy generates short-horizon action trajectories:

::: {.equation-card}
$$
\mathbf{A}_{t:t+H}
\sim
p_\theta(\mathbf{A}_{t:t+H} \mid \mathbf{o}_{t-T_o:t})
$$
:::

The output is not a single command. It is a temporally structured action chunk conditioned on recent observations.

This is exactly the generic diffusion recipe, with the generated sample set to an action chunk and the conditioning set to the observation history:

$$
\mathbf{A}_{t:t+H} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times D_a},
\qquad
c = \mathbf{o}_{t-T_o:t}
$$

---

## From Demonstrations to Training Windows

A long demonstration is converted into many supervised training pairs:

::: {.equation-card}
$$
\left(
\mathbf{o}_{t-T_o+1:t},
\mathbf{A}_{t:t+H-1}
\right)
$$
:::

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
- observation window: recent context
- action chunk: expert future behavior
- slide the window through each episode
- do not cross episode boundaries
:::

::: {}
![](assets/figures/demonstration_windows.svg){width=100%}
:::
:::

---

## Why Action Chunks?

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
- Manipulation is not a sequence of independent actions.
- Contact behavior often depends on short-term history.
- A chunk can commit to one mode among several plausible strategies.
- Replanning keeps the policy closed-loop.
:::

::: {.equation-card}
$$
\mathbf{A}_{t:t+H}
=
\left[
a_t, a_{t+1}, \ldots, a_{t+H}
\right]
$$
:::
:::

---

## PushT as a Concrete Example

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
A minimal PushT setup from the exercise material uses:

- state: agent pose and block pose
- observation: agent position and velocity
- action: relative target displacement
- prediction horizon: a short action chunk

This is enough to explain the data interface without running the notebook.
:::

::: {}
![](assets/figures/pusht_windows.svg){width=100%}
:::
:::

---

## Delta Actions vs Absolute Targets

The same command can be represented as an absolute target or a relative displacement:

::: {.equation-card}
$$
\Delta p_t
=
p^{target}_t
-
p^{agent}_t
$$
:::

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
For PushT-style planar control:

- $p^{agent}_t$: current end-effector or agent position
- $p^{target}_t$: target position from the demonstration
- $\Delta p_t$: action in a local frame
:::

::: {}
![](assets/figures/delta_action_targets.svg){width=100%}
:::
:::

---

## Observation Conditioning

Possible conditioning inputs:

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
- RGB or RGB-D images
- proprioception
- force/torque measurements
- tactile signals
- task labels or language instructions
:::

::: {.takeaway}
Conditioning tells the denoiser which action chunk should emerge from the noise.
:::
:::

---

## Conditional 1D U-Net Denoiser

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
The denoising network receives:

- noisy action chunk $\mathbf{A}_k$
- diffusion step $k$
- observation history $\mathbf{o}_{t-T_o:t}$

It predicts the noise in the action chunk:

$$
\epsilon_\theta(\mathbf{A}_k, k, c)
$$
:::

::: {}
![](assets/figures/diffusion_policy_conditioning.svg){width=100%}
:::
:::

---

## Conditioning with FiLM

FiLM injects context by modulating hidden features:

::: {.equation-card}
$$
h'
=
\gamma(c)\odot h
+
\beta(c)
$$
:::

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
- $h$: intermediate feature map in the denoiser
- $c$: observation and diffusion-step embedding
- $\gamma(c)$: feature-wise scale
- $\beta(c)$: feature-wise shift
:::

::: {}
![](assets/figures/film_modulation.svg){width=100%}
:::
:::

---

## Training Pipeline

::: {.process-list}
1. collect demonstrations
2. build observation windows and expert action chunks
3. normalize observations and actions
4. sample a diffusion step $k$
5. add Gaussian noise to the expert action chunk
6. train the model to predict the injected noise
:::

::: {.equation-card}
$$
\mathcal{L}_{policy}
=
\mathbb{E}
\left[
\lVert
\epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(\mathbf{A}_k, k, \mathbf{o}_{t-T_o:t})
\rVert^2
\right]
$$
:::

---

## Normalization Statistics Travel with the Checkpoint

Deployment must reuse the same statistics used during training:

::: {.equation-card}
$$
\tilde{o}
=
\frac{o-\mu_o}{\sigma_o},
\qquad
\tilde{a}
=
\frac{a-\mu_a}{\sigma_a},
\qquad
a
=
\sigma_a\tilde{a}+\mu_a
$$
:::

::: {.compact}
The deployed policy needs, bundled together as one contract:

- model weights $\theta$
- observation statistics $\mu_o,\sigma_o$
- action statistics $\mu_a,\sigma_a$
- the same action representation used in training
:::

---

## Inference Pipeline

At robot time $t$:

::: {.process-list}
1. encode the current observation history
2. initialize a random action chunk
3. iteratively denoise it with the conditional model
4. denormalize the sampled actions
5. execute a prefix of the chunk
:::

Each denoising step is the conditional reverse update applied to the action chunk:

$$
\mathbf{A}_{k-1}
=
\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_k}}
\left(
\mathbf{A}_k - \frac{1-\alpha_k}{\sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_k}}\,\epsilon_\theta(\mathbf{A}_k, k, \mathbf{o}_{t-T_o:t})
\right)
+ \sqrt{\tilde{\beta}_k}\,z
$$

::: {.takeaway}
The policy constructs an action sequence through iterative refinement rather than a single forward prediction. It is the same DDPM/DDIM reverse loop, now conditioned on observations [@chi2023diffusionpolicy].
:::

---

## Receding Horizon Execution

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
The robot usually does not execute the whole sampled chunk.

- sample $H$ future actions
- execute only the first $h$ actions
- observe again
- sample a new chunk

This makes the policy closed-loop despite sequence outputs.
:::

::: {.equation-card}
$$
\mathbf{A}_{t:t+H}
\rightarrow
\left[
a_t,\ldots,a_{t+h-1}
\right]
\quad\text{then replan}
$$
:::
:::

---

## What This Gives Us

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
Compared with deterministic behavioral cloning, Diffusion Policy gives:

- multimodal continuous-action modeling
- coherent short-horizon plans
- conditioning on rich observations
- a natural bridge to receding horizon execution
:::

::: {.warning}
The price is inference cost. A diffusion policy is a controller only after sampling, timing, and low-level execution are engineered carefully.
:::
:::
